summary: Patrick McKenzie interviews Jonathan Siegel, a Tokyo-based entrepreneur who buys and operates small software businesses as a portfolio. Siegel describes his evolution from building products (with a ~4% hit rate) to acquiring existing businesses, starting with Exceptional and Airbrake in the error-monitoring space. The conversation covers the practical economics of selling small SaaS businesses, including seller financing, typical valuations, and why founders often sell despite ongoing revenue. They discuss separating creative passion from profit-driven discipline, the challenges of scaling teams, and how Silicon Valley culture distorts founders' definitions of success, causing them to reject life-changing acquisition offers in pursuit of billion-dollar outcomes.
key_points: Buying existing small businesses with real customers is far more capital-efficient than starting from scratch, where ~96% of new products fail
Creative passion and profit-driven business should be mentally separated — doing things for passion allows you to be a 'bad businessperson,' while profit requires discipline over creativity
Small SaaS businesses are hard to sell through traditional channels because the transaction costs of packaging a business for sale can exceed the effort that built it
Silicon Valley culture warps founders' definitions of success, causing them to reject meaningful acquisition offers that would be life-changing outside the Bay Area bubble
Running a portfolio of ~5 businesses works when you hire strong teams for day-to-day operations and focus your own time on product and go-to-market strategy
money_quotes: I buy and operate really, really miniscule businesses and I grow them to 'tiny.'
I thought, 'Maybe I'm God's gift to entrepreneuring, because look at all this great stuff that happens when I build stuff.' It turns out I was wrong.

When you have a creative outlet, it's really important to recognize when you are doing things because you need that creative control, and you want to have the passion from it, versus where you want to create profit and actually make money.

I don't even know how many friends of mine in the online software community had a weekend thing and just plugged away at it consistently for four or five years. Suddenly, 'Wait, I woke up in the morning. I've got this thing. I loved it three years ago. I don't love it anymore. But I think it's probably worth more than my house.'
If you don't take that time to write down what success is, then I think you start to become susceptible to other people's idea of success, rather than your own.

Everyone outside of San Francisco is like, 'Oh my God, this is a lottery ticket. I just got a ticket to this kind of wealth creation in such a short amount of time and all I have to do is go work for a great company afterwards?'

When I was 25 I felt like I had all the time in the world and very few high-leverage opportunities, but at 35 I feel like I have lots of high-leverage opportunities but that spending two years doing something is now a decision to be pondered carefully.
Being able to actually ship a software product is still a scarce ability in the world, and even at YC they probably wish they knew more people who could reliably do it.
key_insight: The most reliable path to building wealth through software isn't chasing unicorn outcomes but acquiring and growing small existing businesses while keeping your creative passions separate from your profit-driven discipline.
